content.text: Technical debt in GenAI systems accumulates differently from conventional software debt. Beyond the familiar categories of messy code and missing tests, AI teams carry
prompt debt, model debt, integration debt, and observability debt

— categories that have no analog in a traditional codebase. A technical debt register makes this debt visible, scored, and manageable — turning a vague sense of accumulated shortcuts into a prioritized list of work items with owners and deadlines.
What a Technical Debt Register Is

A technical debt register is a living document that catalogs every known technical shortcut, workaround, and suboptimal design decision in the system — with enough metadata to reason about severity, effort, and priority. It is not a bug tracker (bugs are acute; debt is chronic) and not a roadmap (it captures what needs to be fixed, not what new features to build). Think of it as the team's
maintenance backlog
for systemic problems.
Why GenAI Debt Is Different
Prompt decay:

prompts that worked well six months ago degrade as model versions change, user behavior shifts, and the scope of the feature expands. This decay is invisible in the code — the code hasn't changed, but the output quality has.
Model version lock-in:

teams that pin to a specific model version (e.g., claude-3-haiku-20240307) and never build an upgrade path accumulate model debt. When the pinned version is deprecated, upgrading requires rebuilding and re-evaluating the entire integration.
Evaluation debt:
teams that ship without an offline eval suite have no baseline against which to measure the impact of future changes. Every subsequent model upgrade or prompt change is flying blind.
Observability debt is harder to repay later:
adding trace correlation IDs after a system is in production requires touching every service that makes LLM calls. Retrofitting observability is 5–10× more expensive than building it from the start.
The Four Debt Categories

attrs.alt: GenAI tech debt register: four categories (prompt, model, integration, observability) and register schema

content.text: Prompt Debt
Prompt debt arises when prompts are written for speed of delivery rather than maintainability. Common examples:
Hardcoded few-shot examples that reflect outdated product behavior or edge cases from the original build.
System prompt duplicated across three services with minor variations, so that a change must be applied in multiple places and inconsistencies accumulate over time.
No prompt versioning — the current prompt is stored in an environment variable, and no record exists of what the prompt was three months ago.
Brittle chain-of-thought formatting that breaks when the model produces a slightly different reasoning structure.
Model Debt
Pinned to a model version with a known deprecation date and no plan to upgrade.
No eval suite that would allow a model upgrade to be validated — the team cannot upgrade because they have no way to verify the new model produces equivalent or better outputs.
Single-provider lock-in with no fallback — if the primary provider has an outage, the feature is completely unavailable.
Integration Debt
Synchronous tool calls that block the main thread — should be refactored to async to prevent latency spikes from propagating to the user.
No retry or circuit breaker around tool calls — a single tool failure causes the entire agent turn to fail.
Hardcoded API endpoint URLs in the application code rather than config — changing providers requires a code deployment.
Observability Debt
No trace correlation IDs — cannot link a user request to the LLM call it triggered in logs.
Token count not logged — no visibility into which requests are expensive, preventing cost optimization.
No eval score attached to production traces — cannot detect quality regressions from the observability stack.
Register Schema
Each entry in the register contains the following fields. Keep the schema minimal — a register that requires 20 fields per entry will not be maintained.

# tech-debt-register.yaml — example entries

debt_items:
  - id: DEBT-001
    category: prompt
    title: "Few-shot examples hardcoded in system prompt"
    description: >
      12 few-shot examples are hardcoded in the system prompt string in api/routes/summarize.py.
      Adding, removing, or updating examples requires a code deployment. No version history.
    impact: medium       # quality degradation if examples become stale
    blast_radius: high   # affects all summarization requests
    estimated_effort: 3  # story points
    severity_score: 12   # impact(3) × blast_radius(4); scale 1–25
    owner: "@alice"
    created_date: "2025-01-15"
    target_date: "2025-04-01"
    status: open         # open | in_progress | resolved | dropped
    linked_issues:
      - DEBT-004          # depends on prompt versioning system
    notes: "Blocks any prompt A/B testing until resolved"

  - id: DEBT-002
    category: model
    title: "Pinned to claude-3-haiku-20240307 with no upgrade eval"
    description: >
      Feature is hard-pinned to claude-3-haiku-20240307 in the model_config.py constant.
      No offline eval suite exists that could validate a version upgrade.
      Anthropic has indicated sunset in Q3 2025.
    impact: high
    blast_radius: critical  # total feature outage on deprecation
    estimated_effort: 8
    severity_score: 20
    owner: "@bob"
    created_date: "2025-02-01"
    target_date: "2025-05-15"
    status: in_progress
    notes: "Eval suite in Sprint 19; upgrade attempt Sprint 20"

  - id: DEBT-003
    category: observability
    title: "No trace correlation ID on LLM calls"
    description: >
      LLM calls in the agent loop do not attach the incoming request's trace_id.
      Cannot link a user complaint to the specific LLM call in Datadog.
    impact: medium
    blast_radius: high
    estimated_effort: 2
    severity_score: 8
    owner: "@carol"
    created_date: "2025-01-20"
    target_date: "2025-03-01"
    status: resolved
    resolved_date: "2025-02-28"
Severity Scoring Model
Score each debt item on two axes, then multiply to get a severity score from 1 to 25. Items scoring ≥ 15 are high-priority; items scoring ≥ 20 are critical and should be in the next sprint.
Axis
Impact (quality / reliability degradation if left unresolved)
Negligible
Minor
Medium
High
Critical
Blast Radius (number of users / features affected)
1 feature, < 10 users
1 feature, many users
Multiple features
Most features
All features / total outage

# severity_scoring.py — compute and rank debt items from the register YAML
import yaml
from pathlib import Path
from typing import NamedTuple

IMPACT_SCORES = {"negligible": 1, "minor": 2, "medium": 3, "high": 4, "critical": 5}
BLAST_SCORES = {"low": 1, "medium": 2, "high": 3, "very_high": 4, "critical": 5}

class DebtItem(NamedTuple):
    id: str
    category: str
    title: str
    severity_score: int
    estimated_effort: int
    owner: str
    status: str

def load_and_score_register(path: str = "tech-debt-register.yaml") -> list[DebtItem]:
    data = yaml.safe_load(Path(path).read_text())
    items = []
    for item in data["debt_items"]:
        if item["status"] in ("resolved", "dropped"):
            continue  # skip closed items
        # Recompute severity score from raw axes
        impact = IMPACT_SCORES.get(item.get("impact", "medium"), 3)
        blast = BLAST_SCORES.get(item.get("blast_radius", "medium"), 2)
        score = impact * blast
        items.append(DebtItem(
            id=item["id"],
            category=item["category"],
            title=item["title"],
            severity_score=score,
            estimated_effort=item.get("estimated_effort", 0),
            owner=item.get("owner", "unassigned"),
            status=item["status"],
        ))
    # Sort by severity descending, then effort ascending (quick wins first at equal severity)
    items.sort(key=lambda x: (-x.severity_score, x.estimated_effort))
    return items

def print_register_report(items: list[DebtItem]):
    print(f"{'ID':<10} {'Score':<7} {'Effort':<8} {'Category':<16} {'Owner':<12} Title")
    print("-" * 90)
    for item in items:
        priority = "🔴" if item.severity_score >= 15 else ("🟡" if item.severity_score >= 8 else "🟢")
        print(f"{priority} {item.id:<8} {item.severity_score:<7} {item.estimated_effort:<8} "
              f"{item.category:<16} {item.owner:<12} {item.title[:45]}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    items = load_and_score_register()
    print_register_report(items)
    high_priority = [i for i in items if i.severity_score >= 15]
    print(f"\n{len(high_priority)} high-priority items (score ≥ 15) need sprint allocation")
Worked Examples by Category
Category
Example Debt Item
Impact
Blast Radius
Score
Prompt
Hardcoded few-shot examples that are 18 months old
Medium (3)
High (3)
Model
Pinned model version with 90-day deprecation notice, no eval suite
Critical (5)
Critical (5)
25 — emergency
Integration
Synchronous tool calls blocking main thread
High (4)
High (3)
Observability
No trace correlation IDs on LLM calls
Medium (3)
Very High (4)
Tooling Options

The right tool depends on team size and existing tooling. Consistency matters more than tool choice — a YAML file in the repo that everyone maintains is more valuable than a Notion database nobody updates.
YAML in Git (recommended for < 5 engineers):
version-controlled, diff-reviewable in PRs, CI can validate schema. Simple Python script generates the severity report. Best for teams already living in GitHub.
Linear or Jira (recommended for 5–20 engineers):
debt items appear on the same board as sprint work. Use a tech-debt label and custom fields for category, impact, and blast radius. Sprint allocation is visible in the board view.
Notion database (recommended for cross-functional visibility):
board and table views; easy for non-engineers to read. Loses version history unless Notion's page history is enabled. Good for presenting to stakeholders.
Custom database + dashboard (recommended for > 20 engineers):
a PostgreSQL table with a Grafana or Metabase dashboard enables trend charts (debt accumulation over time by category), severity heatmaps, and owner workload views.
Review Cadence
Monthly triage (30 min):

review all open items. Add new items identified during the month. Re-score items whose context has changed (e.g., deprecation date announced). Identify any new critical items that need immediate sprint allocation.
Quarterly full rescore (90 min):
rescore all open items with fresh context. What was medium impact three months ago may now be high impact as the feature's user base has grown. Identify the top 5 items for the upcoming PI.
Annual purge:
archive resolved items older than 12 months. A register that keeps every resolved item from the past three years becomes unreadable. Move old resolved items to an archive file or archive status.
Common Mistakes
Treating the register as a complaint box:
items without owners, scores, and target dates are wishful thinking, not actionable debt. Every item must have all fields populated before being added.
Not tracking evaluation debt:
teams that record prompt and model debt but not eval debt end up with a register full of items they cannot safely resolve because they have no way to verify that a change is an improvement.
Scoring debt items without updating scores:
a severity score assigned in January 2025 is stale by July 2025 if the product has grown. The quarterly rescore is what makes the register actionable rather than historical.
Never allocating sprint capacity:
a register without sprint allocation is a list of problems, not a managed backlog. See the debt allocation topic for how to connect the register to sprint planning.
Best Practices

Add a debt item during code review when you merge a known shortcut — not six months later when you've forgotten the context. The cost of adding an item immediately is 5 minutes; the cost of reconstructing why the debt exists later is much higher.
Link debt items to the features or components they affect. When that feature is scheduled for development, the linked debt items surface automatically as prerequisites.

Assign one person as the register maintainer (rotating monthly). This person owns the monthly triage and ensures the register stays current. Shared ownership without a named maintainer means the register drifts.
Performance and Scalability

A register with more than 50 open items is unmanageable. When the register exceeds 50 items, run a triage session to drop items that are no longer relevant or merge items that describe the same underlying problem.

Track the register's own health as a metric: open items by category, items older than 90 days with no status change, items with score ≥ 15 and no sprint allocation. A healthy register has < 5 items in the last two categories.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Pros
Cons
Structured register (this approach)
Scored, owned, triaged on cadence; AI categories captured
Requires discipline to maintain; tooling setup investment
Backlog labels only (no register)
Zero setup cost
No severity scoring; no category breakdown; no trend visibility
Confluence page (free-form)
Easy to write; visible to stakeholders
Stale within weeks; no connection to sprint board; unstructured
Security and Reliability

Items describing security vulnerabilities (e.g., missing input sanitization before passing user content to an LLM) should be marked private in the register and tracked in a restricted security backlog — not in the general register visible to all team members.

Treat a model deprecation deadline as a reliability SLA. DEBT items with critical blast radius and a known deprecation date should be escalated to the engineering manager's awareness, not just tracked in the register.
Mental Model
Think of the debt register as a
maintenance log for a physical plant

— a factory or power station. Every piece of aging equipment, deferred maintenance, and temporary workaround is logged with a severity rating and an owner. The plant doesn't shut down to fix every item immediately, but nothing festers untracked. The severity score determines the order in which the maintenance team addresses items, and the quarterly rescore reflects that some old equipment has become more critical as the plant has grown. The four GenAI debt categories (prompt, model, integration, observability) are the four systems in the plant that most commonly develop problems.
Key Takeaways
GenAI teams accumulate four distinct categories of technical debt: prompt debt, model debt, integration debt, and observability debt — each requiring different resolution strategies.
Every register entry needs a severity score (impact × blast radius), an owner, a target date, and a status. Items without these fields are not actionable.
Model deprecation deadlines convert model debt from low-urgency to critical immediately — track them explicitly in the register with the deprecation date as the target date.
The quarterly rescore is essential — a severity score assigned when the feature had 1,000 users is wrong when the feature has 100,000 users. Blast radius grows with the user base.

A YAML file in the Git repository is sufficient for small teams and has the advantage of version control. Migrate to a structured tool when the register exceeds 30 items or the team grows beyond 8 engineers.
Evaluation debt is the most dangerous category: without an eval suite, you cannot safely resolve model debt or prompt debt, because you have no way to verify that a change is an improvement.
